sourceMovie.title: Ghost
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Ghost for the same reason it broke through in 1990: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a thriller rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Jerry Zucker keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The fantasy and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Age of Adaline
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Age of Adaline shares Ghost's gift for fantasy — the 2015 film delivers romance without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Meet Joe Black
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Ghost compelling — the fantasy, the romance — shows up in Meet Joe Black (1998) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that bittersweet storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: What Dreams May Come
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The fantasy energy of Ghost is alive in What Dreams May Come (1998), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a bittersweet payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Horns
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Horns (2013) operates in the same fantasy as Ghost, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The tense payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Law of Desire
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Law of Desire (1987) belongs to the same conversation as Ghost — both anchor their thriller in romance emotion that doesn't let go. The funny undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Defending Your Life
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Ghost's fantasy stayed with you, Defending Your Life (1991) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The romance through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Love Potion No. 9
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Ghost excels in fantasy, Love Potion No. 9 (1992) doubles down on romance to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Chances Are
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Chances Are (1989) is the natural next step after Ghost: same fantasy DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and romance elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Beetlejuice
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Ghost who respond to its fantasy will find Beetlejuice (1988) immediately familiar. Both films share a comedy sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Nightmare Alley
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Ghost fans keep landing on Nightmare Alley (2021) — the thriller is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

faq.question: What should I watch after Ghost?
faq.answer: Try The Age of Adaline, Meet Joe Black, What Dreams May Come—each shares DNA with Ghost while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Ghost?
faq.answer: Ghost (1990) is best described as Fantasy, Thriller, Romance. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Ghost worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this fantasy lane.
